• Broadridge Financial Solutions (NYSE:BR) has launched its Central Risk and Liquidity Optimization Solution, a unified front office platform for trading firms and banks.
  • The platform aims to centralize real time risk management, trade execution, and liquidity optimization across agency and principal trading.
  • The release marks a new stage in Broadridge's capital markets technology offering, targeting fragmented risk tools and disconnected workflows on the sell side.

For investors tracking NYSE:BR, this product arrives after a period of pressure on the share price, with the stock at $155.95 and showing a 29.3% decline year to date and a 33.4% decline over the past year. Longer term performance has been mixed, with returns of 14.3% over three years and 5.1% over five years, highlighting how timing has mattered for holders.

The new platform speaks directly to ongoing demands from trading desks for more integrated risk and liquidity tools, an area where spending and attention remain high across capital markets. The Central Risk and Liquidity Optimization Solution ties together smart order routing, market making, internalization, hedging, and risk monitoring in one place, which speaks directly to sell side pain points around fragmented systems and tighter balance sheet usage. If the platform gains traction with banks, broker dealers and market makers, it could support higher software and services revenue tied to trading volumes and risk usage, rather than one off projects. The recent client wins with Tavira Financial and Matsui Securities show Broadridge is already selling complex trading and post trade infrastructure, so this front office launch slots into an existing franchise that spans order management, connectivity, and SaaS post trade tools. data.title: Garmin’s New Aviator Smartwatch Tests Premium Niche Wearables Thesis
data.description:
• Garmin has introduced the D2 Mach 2 Pro aviator smartwatch with integrated inReach satellite connectivity and aviation specific tools.
  • The watch offers two way satellite messaging, SOS, voice calls, onboard navigation, and in flight biometrics tracking without relying on a phone.
  • The product was unveiled at a major industry expo, underscoring Garmin's focus on pilots and aviation enthusiasts.

For investors watching NYSE:GRMN, this launch adds another specialized product to Garmin's aviation lineup at a time when the stock is trading around $260.8. Over the past year, the shares have returned 33.1%, and the move also comes after a 28.8% return year to date and a 3 year gain of around 7x.

The D2 Mach 2 Pro indicates that Garmin is putting more functionality directly on the wrist for pilots, combining communication, safety, and health tracking into a single device. Readers may want to follow how quickly Garmin expands this kind of integrated connectivity across its broader aviation and outdoor ecosystems, and how pilots respond to phone free communication in real world use.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Garmin by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Garmin.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 3 things going right for Garmin that every investor should see.

The D2 Mach 2 Pro looks like a textbook example of Garmin leaning into high value, niche hardware where Apple, Samsung and other mass market smartwatch makers are less active. By baking in inReach satellite connectivity, LTE, aviation maps and cockpit integration, Garmin is targeting pilots who may be willing to pay a premium for safety, redundancy and convenience on the wrist. At a suggested price of US$1,549.99, each unit has the potential to contribute meaningfully to aviation revenue if adoption is solid, and it also plugs users into higher margin services such as satellite messaging and emergency response. The focus on long battery life, aviation specific features and pilot health monitoring also helps differentiate Garmin within aviation wearables and strengthens switching costs versus other avionics providers such as Honeywell or Collins Aerospace that are more cockpit focused than wrist focused.

  • The launch supports the existing narrative that new aviation products and premium services can be important growth drivers alongside fitness and outdoor wearables.
  • It also raises the bar for execution, as Garmin needs to manage hardware complexity, certification demands and ongoing service reliability to justify the high price point.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on fitness and AI based services, and may not fully reflect how aviation specific wearables and satellite connectivity could influence Garmin's long term revenue mix.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ High product price and a focused pilot audience could limit unit volumes if the watch does not gain traction beyond core aviation enthusiasts.
  • ⚠️ Garmin relies on satellite connectivity and ongoing service delivery, so any outages, regulatory changes or pricing pushback on subscriptions could weigh on adoption.
  • 🎁 The D2 Mach 2 Pro deepens Garmin's position in aviation wearables, a segment where competition from general purpose smartwatch makers is relatively limited.
  • 🎁 Integrated inReach, LTE and aviation tools create more reasons for pilots to stay within Garmin's ecosystem and potentially subscribe to recurring services.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to track early feedback from pilots, attach rates for inReach and other subscription services, and any commentary from management on aviation segment demand following the launch. It is also worth watching how this product sits alongside other new wearables across fitness and outdoor, and whether Garmin continues to roll similar satellite connected features into a broader range of devices. Approach 1: Rexford Industrial Realty Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes Rexford Industrial Realty’s expected adjusted funds from operations in future years and discounts those cash flows back to what they are worth in today’s dollars.

Rexford’s latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about $518.7 million. Based on the current model, projected free cash flow in 2030 is $552.2 million, with interim years shaped by a mix of analyst estimates up to 2030 and then extrapolated figures beyond that. Each of these future cash flows is discounted back using a required return to reflect the risk and the time value of money.

When all those discounted cash flows are added together, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about US$36.18 per share. Compared with the recent share price of around US$36.33, the model suggests Rexford is roughly 0.4% overvalued, which is a very small difference.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links directly to what matters most to equity investors, the earnings that support dividends and potential reinvestment.

A "normal" or "fair" P/E ratio usually reflects what the market expects for future growth and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk tends to point to a lower, more conservative multiple.

Rexford Industrial Realty currently trades on a P/E of about 41.78x. That is above the Industrial REITs industry average of roughly 16.82x and also above the peer group average of about 30.14x. The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company can generate above the return that shareholders require, based on the capital invested in the business. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it asks whether each dollar of equity is earning more than the shareholders’ required return.

For Starwood Property Trust, the model uses a Book Value of $18.34 per share and a Stable EPS of $1.04 per share, based on the median return on equity from the past 5 years. The Cost of Equity is $1.61 per share, which implies an Excess Return of $0.57 per share below that required level. The Average Return on Equity used in the model is 5.83%, with a Stable Book Value estimate of $17.78 per share sourced from four analysts’ future book value estimates.

Putting these inputs together, the Excess Returns valuation implies an intrinsic value of about $7.66 per share. Compared with the recent share price around $18.14, this framework suggests the stock is 136.8% overvalued.

data.title: Companies Like Sierra Nevada Gold (ASX:SNX) Are In A Position To Invest In Growth
data.description:
There's no doubt that money can be made by owning shares of unprofitable businesses. For example, Sierra Nevada Gold (ASX:SNX) shareholders have done very well over the last year, with the share price soaring by 241%. But the harsh reality is that very many loss making companies burn through all their cash and go bankrupt.

So notwithstanding the buoyant share price, we think it's well worth asking whether Sierra Nevada Gold's cash burn is too risky. For the purposes of this article, cash burn is the annual rate at which an unprofitable company spends cash to fund its growth; its negative free cash flow. First, we'll determine its cash runway by comparing its cash burn with its cash reserves.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Does Sierra Nevada Gold Have A Long Cash Runway?

A company's cash runway is calculated by dividing its cash hoard by its cash burn. As at December 2025, Sierra Nevada Gold had cash of US$5.4m and no debt. Importantly, its cash burn was US$2.2m over the trailing twelve months. Therefore, from December 2025 it had 2.5 years of cash runway. Arguably, that's a prudent and sensible length of runway to have. The image below shows how its cash balance has been changing over the last few years.

Check out our latest analysis for Sierra Nevada Gold

How Is Sierra Nevada Gold's Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

Because Sierra Nevada Gold isn't currently generating revenue, we consider it an early-stage business. So while we can't look to sales to understand growth, we can look at how the cash burn is changing to understand how expenditure is trending over time. With the cash burn rate up 8.5% in the last year, it seems that the company is ratcheting up investment in the business over time. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but investors should be mindful of the fact that will shorten the cash runway. Sierra Nevada Gold makes us a little nervous due to its lack of substantial operating revenue. We prefer most of the stocks on this list of stocks that analysts expect to grow.

How Easily Can Sierra Nevada Gold Raise Cash?

While its cash burn is only increasing slightly, Sierra Nevada Gold shareholders should still consider the potential need for further cash, down the track. Issuing new shares, or taking on debt, are the most common ways for a listed company to raise more money for its business. One of the main advantages held by publicly listed companies is that they can sell shares to investors to raise cash and fund growth. By comparing a company's annual cash burn to its total market capitalisation, we can estimate roughly how many shares it would have to issue in order to run the company for another year (at the same burn rate).

Sierra Nevada Gold's cash burn of US$2.2m is about 11% of its US$20m market capitalisation. As a result, we'd venture that the company could raise more cash for growth without much trouble, albeit at the cost of some dilution.

Is Sierra Nevada Gold's Cash Burn A Worry?

As you can probably tell by now, we're not too worried about Sierra Nevada Gold's cash burn. For example, we think its cash runway suggests that the company is on a good path. Although its increasing cash burn does give us reason for pause, the other metrics we discussed in this article form a positive picture overall. Based on the factors mentioned in this article, we think its cash burn situation warrants some attention from shareholders, but we don't think they should be worried. Taking a deeper dive, we've spotted 4 warning signs for Sierra Nevada Gold you should be aware of, and 2 of them are a bit unpleasant.

data.title: We Like The Quality Of Gendai Agency's (TSE:2411) Earnings
data.description:
Gendai Agency Inc.'s (TSE:2411) recent earnings report didn't offer any surprises, with the shares unchanged over the last week. Our analysis suggests that shareholders might be missing some positive underlying factors in the earnings report.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against Gendai Agency's Earnings

As finance nerds would already know, the accrual ratio from cashflow is a key measure for assessing how well a company's free cash flow (FCF) matches its profit. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

That means a negative accrual ratio is a good thing, because it shows that the company is bringing in more free cash flow than its profit would suggest. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

For the year to March 2026, Gendai Agency had an accrual ratio of -0.12. Therefore, its statutory earnings were quite a lot less than its free cashflow. To wit, it produced free cash flow of JP¥618m during the period, dwarfing its reported profit of JP¥473.0m. Gendai Agency's free cash flow improved over the last year, which is generally good to see. However, that's not all there is to consider. We can see that unusual items have impacted its statutory profit, and therefore the accrual ratio.

Check out our latest analysis for Gendai Agency

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Gendai Agency.

How Do Unusual Items Influence Profit?

Surprisingly, given Gendai Agency's accrual ratio implied strong cash conversion, its paper profit was actually boosted by JP¥61m in unusual items. We can't deny that higher profits generally leave us optimistic, but we'd prefer it if the profit were to be sustainable. We ran the numbers on most publicly listed companies worldwide, and it's very common for unusual items to be once-off in nature. And, after all, that's exactly what the accounting terminology implies. Assuming those unusual items don't show up again in the current year, we'd thus expect profit to be weaker next year (in the absence of business growth, that is).

Our Take On Gendai Agency's Profit Performance

In conclusion, Gendai Agency's accrual ratio suggests its statutory earnings are of good quality, but on the other hand the profits were boosted by unusual items. Based on these factors, it's hard to tell if Gendai Agency's profits are a reasonable reflection of its underlying profitability. With this in mind, we wouldn't consider investing in a stock unless we had a thorough understanding of the risks. While conducting our analysis, we found that Gendai Agency has 2 warning signs and it would be unwise to ignore these.

Our examination of Gendai Agency has focussed on certain factors that can make its earnings look better than they are. But there are plenty of other ways to inform your opinion of a company. Valley National Bancorp Q1 2026 earnings snapshot

Valley National Bancorp (VLY) opened 2026 with Q1 results that build on its recent momentum, coming off Q4 2025 total revenue of US$505.9 million and basic EPS of US$0.34, supported by trailing 12 month net income of US$569.0 million and basic EPS of US$1.02. The bank has seen total revenue move from US$365.9 million in Q4 2024 to US$505.9 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly basic EPS stepped up from US$0.20 to US$0.34 over the same period. This sets up Q1 2026 against a backdrop of expanding profitability. With trailing net profit margins and dividends both in focus, investors are likely to read the latest earnings through the lens of how durable these margin levels appear.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to line them up against the most common narratives around Valley National Bancorp to see which stories fit the data and which ones start to look stretched.

Margins and costs point to efficiency gains

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Valley National Bancorp reported a net profit margin of 30.9%, with a cost to income ratio of 54.44% and net interest margin of 3.05%, compared with a 2.85% net interest margin and 57.98% cost to income ratio for the trailing 12 months a year earlier.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative expects digitalization and operational efficiency to support margins. The recent move from a 2.85% to 3.05% net interest margin and from a 57.98% to 54.44% cost to income ratio provides concrete data for that view, although it also sets a higher bar for maintaining these levels over time.
    • Supporters of the bullish angle on efficiency can point to trailing 12 month net income of US$569.0 million on US$1.8b of revenue as evidence that the current margin structure is already delivering stronger profitability than in the previous year.
    • At the same time, the consensus narrative flags rising compliance and technology costs as an ongoing risk, so any reversal in the cost to income ratio from the current 54.44% level would be important for investors tracking whether the efficiency story is playing out as expected.

Strong recent margin and earnings trends are exactly what bullish investors reference when they talk about Valley's profit story. The dedicated bull case sets out how those themes could play out over time for the business 🐂 Valley National Bancorp Bull Case.

Credit quality: non performing loans creeping higher

  • Non performing loans on a trailing 12 month basis sit at US$433.9 million compared with US$359.5 million a year earlier and US$296.3 million in the period ending Q3 2024, against a loan book that is around US$50.1b.
  • Bears focus on credit risk, especially commercial real estate, and the steady move in non performing loans from US$296.3 million to US$433.9 million gives them specific numbers to point to when questioning how resilient recent earnings and margins might be.
    • Critics highlight that ongoing exposure to commercial real estate and region specific shocks could require higher provisions, and the rise in non performing loans over the last few reported periods is consistent with that concern.
    • On the other hand, the consensus narrative notes that specialized verticals such as healthcare and fund finance have shown low credit losses, so investors may want to separate pockets of stress from areas where asset quality has been described as relatively stable.

For readers who want to see how the cautious credit view stacks up against the numbers sector by sector, the detailed bear case breaks that argument out in full 🐻 Valley National Bancorp Bear Case.

Valuation tension: higher P/E and DCF gap

  • With a current share price of US$13.77, Valley National Bancorp trades on a trailing P/E of 13.4x versus peer and US Banks industry averages of 12.8x and 11.7x respectively, while a DCF fair value of about US$27.50 and an analyst price target of US$14.96 both sit above the current market level.
  • Consensus narrative supporters point to the mix of earnings growth, a 3.2% dividend yield, and the DCF fair value gap as reasons the current price could be out of step with fundamentals, while the higher than peer P/E keeps the discussion grounded in what investors are actually paying today.
    • The trailing 12 month basic EPS of US$1.02 is what underpins both the 13.4x P/E and the DCF fair value of US$27.50, so any shift in earnings would directly affect both sides of that valuation tension.
    • Meanwhile, the analyst price target of US$14.96 is close to the current US$13.77 price, which fits with the idea that, despite a higher P/E than peers, the stock is viewed as roughly fairly valued when forecasts and risks are taken into account.

data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Axon Enterprise at around US$392.64 is starting to look compelling, or if the price already reflects its strengths, this article walks through the key valuation angles that matter for you.
  • The stock has slipped 0.1% over the last 7 days, 14.0% over the last 30 days, 30.3% year to date, and 33.5% over the last year, even after a 3 year return of 82.2% and a 5 year return of 154.9%.

Axon Enterprise scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Axon Enterprise Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects the cash Axon Enterprise could generate in the future and then discounts those cash flows back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars.

Axon’s latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $92.5 million. Based on analyst inputs and extrapolations, future Free Cash Flow projections reach $455.96 million in 2026 and $966.77 million in 2028, with further estimates extending out to 2035. These figures are expressed in $ and use a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model that blends analyst forecasts with longer term assumptions provided by Simply Wall St.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted to today, the resulting estimated intrinsic value comes out at about $375.52 per share. Compared with the recent share price of around $392.64, the model indicates the stock is about 4.6% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Axon Enterprise is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Axon Enterprise.

Approach 2: Axon Enterprise Price vs Sales

For companies that are already generating meaningful revenue, the P/S ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of sales, especially when earnings can be influenced by accounting items or reinvestment decisions.

What counts as a reasonable P/S depends on how quickly investors expect revenue to grow and how risky the business is perceived to be. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually call for a lower one.

Axon Enterprise currently trades on a P/S of about 11.38x. That sits above the Aerospace & Defense industry average of roughly 5.02x and also above the peer average of about 7.79x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework estimates a P/S of around 17.26x for Axon Enterprise, based on factors such as its growth profile, profit margins, industry, market value and risk characteristics. Because this Fair Ratio is tailored to the company’s specific fundamentals, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the industry.

Comparing the current P/S of 11.38x with the Fair Ratio of 17.26x indicates that the shares are trading below that modelled level.
